title: MODELS_WITHOUT_TENANT list is hardcoded and incomplete
description: The set of models that should bypass tenant filtering only contains 'Tenant' and 'VerificationToken'. However, other platform-level models likely exist (e.g., SiteSetting, AdminConfig, etc.). A missed model means platform data gets wrongly filtered by tenantId, causing silent data loss. This list must be kept in sync with the Prisma schema manually — there's no validation.
fix: Generate the MODELS_WITHOUT_TENANT set automatically from the Prisma schema by checking which models have a tenantId field. Add a build-time validation step that compares the set against the actual schema.

title: 47+ module-level Map instances used as in-memory state — breaks in serverless/multi-instance
description: Found 47+ module-level Map() declarations used for state (rate limits, sessions, caches, call state). Key files: mobile-guard.ts:40, session-security.ts:15-16/118, brute-force-protection.ts:19, security.ts:34/433, email-service.ts:155, rate-limiter.ts:121, cache.ts:131-132, crm/payment-ivr.ts:54, voip/call-control.ts:29-39, voip/voice-ai-engine.ts:72, voip/messaging-channel.ts:82, accounting/search.service.ts:417, crm/streaming-transcription.ts:53, and many more. In serverless environments (Azure App Service with multiple instances), this state is not shared between instances, causing: split-brain rate limiting, session inconsistency, missed call routing. Some files have Redis fallback (rate-limiter, brute-force) but many don't.
fix: For state that must be shared: use Redis consistently (already available). For state that is instance-local by design (caches): document this explicitly and ensure TTLs are set. Critical: voip/crm Maps for call state MUST use Redis or a shared store when scaling beyond a single instance.

title: Extensive use of 'any' type (42+ occurrences) weakens type safety
description: Found 42+ instances of ': any', 'as any', or 'any[]' across src/lib/. Key locations: storage.ts:48/50/167/223/254/610, auth-config.ts:241/271, telnyx.ts:308/330, crm/offline-sync.ts:462/469/477/485, crm/bi-connector.ts:213/242, rate-limiter.ts:92/97, redis.ts:73/78, voip/livekit-service.ts:10, voip/livekit-recording.ts:9, sanitize.ts:86, ads/ads-sync.ts:364, voip/connection.ts:202, voip/live-sentiment.ts:330, crm/payment-ivr.ts:144, admin-api-guard.ts:37/129. Many are marked with eslint-disable comments, indicating awareness but no fix.
fix: Replace 'any' with proper types: use import('module').Type for lazy-loaded modules, use Prisma.TransactionClient for transaction parameters, use Record<string, unknown> for dynamic objects, and create proper interface types for webhook payloads.

title: require() used instead of dynamic import() in 19 files
description: Found require() calls in 19 files: validation.ts:49, voip/transcription.ts:37, apm.ts:46, crm/payment-ivr.ts:159, crm/contact-enrichment.ts:24, crm/ai-coaching.ts:24, crm/realtime-sentiment.ts:24, crm/voice-ai-agent.ts:29, crm/domain-warmup.ts:38, crm/ai-quality-monitor.ts:27, crm/ivr-speech-recognition.ts:39, crm/mms.ts:58, crm/business-card-scanner.ts:26, crm/email-sync.ts:40, crm/agent-wellness.ts:38, crm/chatbot-engine.ts:39, crm/ai-quality-evaluation.ts:39, crm/conversation-intelligence.ts:24, crm/form-shortening-ai.ts:36, crm/whatsapp.ts:30/296. require() bypasses TypeScript type checking and tree shaking. Most of these are for lazy-loading SDKs but should use the async import() pattern consistently.
fix: Replace all require() calls with dynamic import(). For example: const { default: OpenAI } = await import('openai') instead of const { default: OpenAI } = require('openai').

title: globalCacheStore has no max size limit — unbounded memory growth
description: The in-memory L1 cache (globalCacheStore Map on line 131) has no maximum size enforcement. While entries have TTLs, under high traffic with diverse cache keys, the Map can grow unboundedly before entries expire. The tagIndex Map (line 132) also grows without bounds. Contrast with session-security.ts which has SESSION_CACHE_MAX_SIZE = 10,000 and eviction logic.
fix: Add a MAX_CACHE_SIZE constant and enforceCacheMaxSize() function similar to session-security.ts. Evict oldest entries when the limit is reached. Also periodically sweep expired entries.

title: MessagingChannel stores conversations in unbounded in-memory Map
description: The MessagingChannel class stores all conversation messages in a Map<string, Message[]> (line 82). Messages are only added, never evicted. Over time this grows without bound, especially on high-volume messaging channels. Lines 272 and 315 return copies of the entire Map which compounds the memory issue.
fix: Add a maximum conversation count and maximum messages per conversation. Implement LRU eviction or TTL-based cleanup. For production use, store conversations in the database and only cache recent messages.

title: APM metricsBuffer array grows to 10,000 entries without flush mechanism
description: The metricsBuffer array on line 127 has MAX_BUFFER_SIZE = 10,000 (line 128) but there is no visible periodic flush mechanism. When full, new entries likely overwrite or are dropped, but the 10,000-entry buffer persists in memory permanently. This is a memory holding pattern that never releases.
fix: Implement a periodic flush (e.g., every 60 seconds) that either ships metrics to an external system (Sentry, custom endpoint) or rotates the buffer. Use a circular buffer pattern instead of an array that grows to max size.

title: Saved searches stored in unbounded in-memory Map, lost on restart
description: The savedSearches Map on line 417 stores user-created saved searches in process memory. These are lost on every restart/deploy. There's no persistence to database and no size limit. Users may create saved searches thinking they're persistent.
fix: Persist saved searches to the database (create a SavedSearch Prisma model). Use the Map only as a cache with TTL and max size.

title: 4 unbounded Maps for active call state with no cleanup on crash
description: Four module-level Maps store per-call AI state: activeAgentAssist (line 29), activeScorers (line 32), activeSentiment (line 35), activeConversationalIVR (line 39). If a call ends abnormally (crash, timeout, network issue), the Map entries are never cleaned up. Over time with many calls, these Maps accumulate zombie entries holding AI model state and conversation history in memory.
fix: Add a periodic cleanup sweep (e.g., every 5 minutes) that removes entries for calls that are no longer active (check VoipStateMap). Also add a maxSize limit with LRU eviction.

title: Code duplication: sanitizeName() function duplicated in 3+ email sending functions
description: The sanitizeName function (strips CRLF for header injection prevention) is independently defined as a local function inside sendViaResend (line 513), sendViaSendGrid (line 572), and sendViaSMTP (line 667) in email-service.ts. The same CRLF-stripping pattern appears in at least 3 more places. This is a maintenance risk — if the sanitization logic needs updating, it must be changed in all locations.
fix: Extract sanitizeName into a shared utility function in @/lib/sanitize or @/lib/email/utils and import it in all locations.

file: src/lib/ (security.ts, session-security.ts, rate-limiter.ts, mobile-guard.ts, brute-force-protection.ts, email-service.ts)
title: In-memory Map eviction logic duplicated in 6+ files
description: The pattern of enforcing a max Map size with eviction of oldest entries is independently implemented in: session-security.ts (enforceCacheMaxSize), security.ts (enforceRateLimitStoreMaxSize), rate-limiter.ts (enforceRateLimitCacheMaxSize), brute-force-protection.ts (enforceMapSizeLimit), email-service.ts (evictRateLimitCache). Each has slightly different logic (evict half vs evict 1000 extra). The mobile-guard.ts rateLimitMap has NO eviction at all.
fix: Create a generic BoundedMap<K,V> utility class that encapsulates the max-size eviction pattern. All files should use this shared implementation for consistency.

title: AI config defaults apiKey to empty string — fails open if env not set
description: The DEFAULT_CONFIG on line 44 sets apiKey: '' as default. If OPENAI_API_KEY is not set in the environment and no apiKey is passed to the constructor, API calls will fail with an auth error rather than being explicitly blocked. The empty string default masks the misconfiguration. Same pattern in voip/agent-assist.ts:48.
fix: Throw an explicit error when apiKey is empty at the point of first API call. Replace empty string default with undefined and add validation: if (!this.config.apiKey) throw new Error('OpenAI API key not configured').

title: Stripe SDK loaded via require() without version pinning in payment-critical code
description: Line 159 uses `const Stripe = require('stripe')` inside a function — lazy loading, which is good. However, require() bypasses TypeScript's type system entirely for this security-critical payment processing code. Any version mismatch or API change would fail at runtime with no type safety.
fix: Use: const { default: Stripe } = await import('stripe') for proper type checking. Pin the Stripe SDK version in package.json.

title: Credentials provider authorize() returns user object 'as any'
description: Line 241 casts the returned user object as 'as any' to satisfy NextAuth's type system. This bypasses type checking for the authentication return value — the most security-critical function in the codebase. If the User type changes (e.g., adding required fields for tenant isolation), TypeScript won't catch the mismatch.
fix: Define a proper return type that extends NextAuth's User type with the additional fields (role, mfaEnabled) and use 'satisfies' instead of 'as any'.

title: Fire-and-forget audit log in auth flow swallows all errors silently
description: Line 173 creates an audit log entry for brute force lockout with .catch(() => {}), silently swallowing any error. While the comment says 'Fire-and-forget, don't block auth' — if the audit log consistently fails (e.g., DB connection issue), security events are permanently lost with no alerting. This applies to security-critical events that compliance requires to be logged.
fix: Add at minimum a logger.error in the catch: .catch((e) => logger.error('Audit log write failed for lockout event', { error: e.message })). Consider a secondary logging mechanism (file-based or queue) for critical security events.

title: Session security relies on in-memory cache — sessions survive across server restarts in different state
description: The session validity system uses three in-memory Maps (lines 15-16, 118) for session tracking. On server restart, all session metadata is lost. This means: (1) sessions appear as 'not_found' (line 69) rather than properly expired, (2) the inactivity timeout resets on every deploy, (3) anomaly detection (IP/UA changes) has no memory of previous sessions. The system is meant to be NYDFS-compliant but cannot enforce session policies across restarts.
fix: Store session activity timestamps and metadata in Redis (already available in the project) instead of in-memory Maps. The isSessionValid check should query Redis for the last activity time.

title: Module-level setInterval for session cleanup runs unconditionally
description: Line 340-341 starts a setInterval that runs cleanupExpiredSessions every 5 minutes. This interval is never cleared — it runs for the entire process lifetime. On line 332-336, the cleanup function does a dynamic import and Prisma query (deleteMany) which can fail silently. The interval reference is not tracked, so graceful shutdown cannot clean it up.
fix: Track the interval reference and add a cleanup function for graceful shutdown. Register with process.on('SIGTERM'). Consider using the cron-lock.ts system instead for periodic cleanup.

title: Mobile rate limit Map has no size limit — DoS vector
description: The rateLimitMap on line 40 has no maximum size enforcement. Unlike other rate limit Maps in the codebase (which have enforceMapSizeLimit()), this one grows unboundedly. An attacker could send requests from thousands of unique IPs/tokens to fill the Map and cause memory exhaustion. The cleanup interval on line 56-61 only removes expired entries, not entries beyond a max count.
fix: Add a MAX_SIZE constant and enforceMapSizeLimit() function, similar to brute-force-protection.ts. Evict oldest entries when the Map exceeds the limit.

title: Account lockout notification email includes raw IP address — potential information disclosure
description: Lines 328-343 generate an HTML email that includes the raw ipAddress in the template. If this email is forwarded or viewed by non-technical users, the IP information could be sensitive. Additionally, the email HTML is constructed via string interpolation without HTML-encoding the variables (email, ipAddress, siteName), which could be a stored XSS vector if the email is displayed in a web-based email client.
fix: HTML-encode all interpolated values in the email template using escapeHtml() from @/lib/security. Consider showing a truncated/anonymized IP in user-facing emails and the full IP only in admin audit logs.

title: AI copilot uses hardcoded model identifiers that may become deprecated
description: Lines 52-55 hardcode AI model identifiers: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514', 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001', 'gpt-4o', 'gpt-4o-mini'. These model IDs can be deprecated by the providers without notice, causing the copilot to fail. There's no fallback mechanism if a specific model is unavailable.
fix: Move model identifiers to environment variables (e.g., COPILOT_MODEL, COPILOT_FAST_MODEL) with the current values as defaults. Add error handling that falls back to a known-working model if the primary model returns a not-found error.

title: setInterval for rate limit cleanup runs unconditionally — timer leak
description: Line 457 creates a setInterval for cleanup that runs every 5 minutes. While line 454 stores the reference and line 511-516 provides a cleanup function registered on SIGTERM, the timer is created at module load time and runs in every context (including test environments). The comment on line 7 acknowledges this as FAILLE-087.
fix: Create a central timer registry as suggested by the TODO. Only start timers when explicitly initialized (e.g., during server startup), not at module import time. This prevents timers from running in test environments and build processes.

title: StorageService uses process.cwd() for upload directory — Azure read-only filesystem issue
description: Line 85 sets localUploadDir to path.join(process.cwd(), 'public', 'uploads'). On Azure App Service with WEBSITE_RUN_FROM_PACKAGE=1, process.cwd() points to a read-only mounted filesystem. This is documented as KB-PP-BUILD-006. While Azure uploads go through the Azure Blob path, the local fallback would fail in production if Azure Storage is misconfigured.
fix: Use /tmp/ for the local fallback directory on Azure, or detect the environment and use an appropriate writable path. Add a startup check that verifies the upload directory is writable.

title: Magic byte validation is incomplete — missing SVG and video MIME types
description: The MAGIC_SIGNATURES map (line 28) only covers JPEG, PNG, GIF, WebP, PDF, BMP, and TIFF. There's no validation for video types that the admin context might allow. More critically, SVG is blocked by extension (line 79) but if a file were uploaded with a whitelisted extension and SVG content, the magic byte check would fail to detect it (SVG is XML-based with no unique magic bytes). The validateMagicBytes function returns false for unknown MIME types (line 174) which is the correct behavior but should be documented.
fix: Add magic byte signatures for additional allowed types as they're added to ALLOWED_TYPES_BY_CONTEXT. For SVG/XML detection, add a content-based check that looks for XML prologs or <svg> tags in files claiming to be images. Document the fail-closed behavior for unknown MIME types.

title: sanitizeWebhookPayload only handles known payload structures
description: The sanitizeWebhookPayload function (line 86) handles Stripe charges.data and PayPal resource structures, but only deletes known sensitive field names at the top level. Deeply nested structures or new webhook formats could contain sensitive data that isn't stripped. The function accepts 'any' input and returns 'any' output.
fix: Implement a recursive sanitization that strips known-sensitive field names at all nesting levels. Add a whitelist approach: define which fields ARE allowed rather than which fields to delete. Add proper TypeScript types instead of 'any'.

title: OpenAI require() pattern repeated in 11 CRM files without centralized initialization
description: The pattern `const { default: OpenAI } = require('openai')` appears in 11 CRM files: contact-enrichment.ts, ai-coaching.ts, realtime-sentiment.ts, voice-ai-agent.ts, domain-warmup.ts, ai-quality-monitor.ts, ivr-speech-recognition.ts, business-card-scanner.ts, agent-wellness.ts, chatbot-engine.ts, ai-quality-evaluation.ts, conversation-intelligence.ts, form-shortening-ai.ts. Each creates its own OpenAI instance. No centralized API key validation, usage tracking, or error handling.
fix: Create a centralized @/lib/openai.ts that exports a lazy-loaded getOpenAI() function (like the pattern in copilot-service.ts and openai-chat.ts). All CRM files should import from this central module. This enables: single API key validation, usage tracking, cost monitoring, and model version management.

title: Ad campaign upsert is N+1 — one DB query per campaign per day
description: The upsertSnapshots function (line 52) iterates over campaigns and issues one prisma.upsert per campaign (line 55). For 30 days of data across multiple campaigns, this could mean hundreds of individual DB round-trips. Other parts of the codebase have been fixed with $transaction batch patterns but this one was missed.
fix: Batch the upserts using prisma.$transaction([...]) with an array of upsert operations, similar to the N+1 fixes in voip/webhook-dispatcher.ts and crm/email-signature-manager.ts.

title: Module-level setInterval timers created on import — wastes resources in build/test
description: Six files create setInterval timers at module import time: mobile-guard.ts:56, session-security.ts:341, security.ts:457, brute-force-protection.ts:303-304, rate-limiter.ts:477-478, crm/payment-ivr.ts:57. Most are guarded by `typeof setInterval !== 'undefined'` but they still run during development, testing, and build processes. Each timer holds a reference that prevents garbage collection of the enclosing module scope.
fix: Create a central timer registry (as noted in FAILLE-087). Only start timers when the server is actually running requests, not on module import. Provide a cleanup function for graceful shutdown that clears all timers.

title: Derived key cache has no TTL — scrypt keys persist for process lifetime
description: The derivedKeyCache Map (line 34) caches scrypt-derived keys by salt hex string. Max 200 entries with LRU eviction. However, there's no TTL on cached entries — a key derived at startup will remain cached for the entire process lifetime. This is a minor security concern: if ENCRYPTION_KEY is rotated, old derived keys remain valid in cache until the process restarts or the entry is evicted by LRU.
fix: Add a TTL (e.g., 1 hour) to cached entries. Store { key: Buffer, cachedAt: number } and check age on access. This ensures key rotation takes effect within the TTL period.

title: Email rate limit Map has passive eviction — stale entries accumulate
description: The emailRateMap (line 155) only evicts expired entries every 1000 calls (line 206). Between eviction sweeps, stale entries from unique email addresses accumulate in memory. Under normal load with diverse email recipients, thousands of entries could build up. Unlike the rate-limiter.ts which has a proactive setInterval cleanup, this one relies solely on access-pattern-driven eviction.
fix: Add a periodic cleanup interval (similar to rate-limiter.ts) or integrate with the central rate-limiter instead of maintaining a separate Map.

title: Payment session cleanup runs every 60 seconds even when no sessions exist
description: The setInterval on line 57 iterates over all sessions every 60 seconds, even when the sessions Map is empty. This is a waste of CPU cycles and, more importantly, this interval is not tracked and cannot be cleaned up on shutdown.
fix: Only start the cleanup interval when the first session is created. Clear the interval when the last session is removed. Track the interval reference for graceful shutdown.

title: IP change detection logs full IP addresses — GDPR concern
description: Lines 148-155 and 159-165 log full IP addresses and user agents when session anomalies are detected. IP addresses are PII under GDPR. The logging should mask/hash the IPs or use a privacy-preserving comparison method.
fix: Hash or partially mask IP addresses in log output. For comparison, hash the IPs and compare hashes. Log only the country/ASN information for anomaly context.

title: Dynamic Prisma model access via (prisma as any)[model] — unsafe type assertion
description: Lines 462, 469, 477, 485 use (prisma as any)[model] to dynamically access Prisma models by string name. This bypasses all TypeScript type checking and allows arbitrary model access. If the 'model' parameter comes from user input (even indirectly), it could access sensitive models like User, Account, or Session. Same pattern in crm/bi-connector.ts:213/242 and auditors/azure-local-sync.ts:380/397.
fix: Create a whitelist of allowed model names and validate 'model' against it before accessing. Use a proper type-safe model accessor: type PrismaModel = 'lead' | 'contact' | 'deal'; const models: Record<PrismaModel, typeof prisma.lead> = { ... }.

title: 25+ untracked setTimeout/setInterval calls in voip/ services
description: The voip/ directory contains 25+ setTimeout and setInterval calls across files: call-control.ts:748, power-dialer.ts:236/285, simultaneous-ring.ts:176, missed-call-followup.ts:85, dnd-manager.ts:288, call-park.ts:103, call-pickup.ts:53, deepgram-stt.ts:297, call-quality-monitor.ts:143, vad-analytics.ts:126, streaming-transcription.ts:133/173/182, video-recording.ts:104, krisp-noise-cancel.ts:271, calendar-sync.ts:268, audit-log.ts:304, voice-ai-engine.ts:599, incoming-notification.ts:114. Many of these timers are not properly cleaned up if the call ends unexpectedly, leading to timer leaks and potential use-after-cleanup errors.
fix: Create a central timer registry for the VoIP subsystem that tracks all active timers by callId. Implement a cleanup function that clears all timers for a given call. Call this cleanup on every call termination event (call.hangup, error, timeout).

file: src/lib/ (crm/sms-link-tracking.ts, crm/sms-drip-sequence.ts, crm/sms-surveys.ts, crm/post-call-survey.ts, crm/conference-call.ts, crm/acd-engine.ts, crm/call-blending.ts, crm/agentless-dialer.ts, crm/dialer-modes.ts, crm/noise-cancellation.ts)
title: 10+ CRM service files use in-memory Maps for persistent state that should be in DB/Redis
description: Multiple CRM service files store operational state in module-level Maps that is logically persistent (SMS sequences, survey enrollments, queue state, conference info, etc.): sms-link-tracking.ts:54, sms-drip-sequence.ts:58-59, sms-surveys.ts:67, post-call-survey.ts:65, conference-call.ts:47, acd-engine.ts:65, call-blending.ts:32, agentless-dialer.ts:43, dialer-modes.ts:41, noise-cancellation.ts:102. All this state is lost on server restart, which could cause: missed SMS sequence steps, lost survey responses, dropped queued calls, stale conference state.
fix: For state that must survive restarts (SMS sequences, survey enrollments): persist to PostgreSQL. For ephemeral state that needs sharing across instances (active calls, queues): use Redis. For truly instance-local state (noise cancellation per-call): keep in-memory but add cleanup.

title: Telnyx API key read from process.env without validation on every request
description: The MessagingChannel constructor (line 84-90) reads TELNYX_API_KEY from process.env on every instantiation but doesn't validate it's present. If the env var is missing, API calls will fail with a confusing auth error rather than a clear configuration error.
fix: Add explicit validation: if (!this.config.telnyxApiKey) throw new Error('TELNYX_API_KEY not configured'). Add this check at construction time, not first use.

title: User name and product name interpolated directly into email HTML templates
description: Lines 998, 1406, 1487, 1523 interpolate safeName, safeProductName, and URLs directly into HTML template strings. While the variables use 'safe' prefix suggesting they're sanitized elsewhere, if the sanitization is incomplete (e.g., missing entities for angle brackets), this is a stored XSS vector in sent emails.
fix: Verify that safeName and safeProductName are sanitized with the full escapeHtml() function from @/lib/security. Add a HTML template engine with automatic escaping instead of manual string interpolation.

title: FX rate cache has no size limit or periodic cleanup
description: The rateCache Map on line 18 stores forex rates with a TTL-per-entry pattern but has no maximum size limit and no periodic cleanup sweep. If queried for many currency pairs over time, the cache grows without bound. Same issue in accounting/currency.service.ts:37.
fix: Add MAX_CACHE_SIZE eviction and a periodic cleanup sweep for expired entries.

title: Conversation memory cache grows without bounds
description: The memoryCache Map on line 49 stores ConversationState for voice AI conversations. These contain conversation history and context. No maximum size limit, no periodic cleanup.
fix: Add MAX_CACHE_SIZE, LRU eviction, and TTL-based cleanup.

title: Azure Key Vault secret cache has no periodic cleanup
description: The secretCache Map on line 126 stores secrets with per-entry TTL but no periodic sweep to remove expired entries. Between accesses, expired entries consume memory unnecessarily.
fix: Add a periodic cleanup interval or use a bounded cache with LRU eviction.

title: AI accountant chat sessions stored in unbounded in-memory Map
description: The sessions Map on line 1400 stores ChatSession objects for the AI accountant chatbot. These contain conversation history that grows per message. No maximum session count, no message limit per session, no automatic cleanup.
fix: Add MAX_SESSIONS limit, per-session message cap, and TTL-based cleanup. Consider persisting chat history to the database for audit and continuity.

title: IVR sessions stored in unbounded Map with potential PII from voice input
description: The ivrSessions Map on line 137 stores IvrSession objects that include speech recognition results. These may contain PII from caller voice input (spoken names, account numbers, etc.). No size limit, no TTL, no PII redaction.
fix: Add TTL-based cleanup (IVR sessions should expire within minutes). Implement PII redaction for stored speech recognition results. Add MAX_SESSIONS limit.

title: Counters and histograms Maps grow without bounds
description: The counters and histograms Maps on lines 39-40 store metric data points. With high-cardinality metric names (e.g., per-route, per-user), these Maps grow unboundedly.
fix: Add MAX_METRICS_COUNT and implement eviction of least-recently-updated metrics. Alternatively, implement periodic aggregation that compresses old data points.

title: Media fork sessions Map stores audio stream references without cleanup
description: The activeForks Map on line 36 stores MediaForkSession objects that reference active audio streams. If a call ends without proper cleanup, these sessions hold references to network resources that may never be released.
fix: Add automatic cleanup of fork sessions when the associated call ends. Implement a periodic sweep for stale sessions (e.g., older than 1 hour).

title: LiveKit SDK instances stored as 'any' type
description: Line 10 declares `let _roomService: any = null`. Similarly voip/livekit-recording.ts:9 declares `let _egressClient: any = null`. Using 'any' for SDK instances means no autocomplete, no type checking on method calls, and no compile-time error detection for API changes.
fix: Use proper import types: import type { RoomServiceClient } from 'livekit-server-sdk'. Cast correctly after dynamic import.

title: Backup container client stored as 'any' type — security-critical storage
description: Line 48 declares `let backupContainerClient: any = null`. The backup storage client handles database backup files. Using 'any' means no type safety for operations on backup data, which could lead to accidentally exposing or corrupting backups.
fix: Use proper Azure Blob Storage types via import type.

title: Active transcription/sentiment Maps store streaming data without bounds
description: crm/streaming-transcription.ts:53 (activeTranscriptions Map) and crm/realtime-sentiment.ts:258 (_activeSessions Map) store streaming transcription and sentiment data for live calls. These grow per-call and per-utterance with no maximum size.
fix: Add per-session transcript size limits, MAX_SESSIONS cap, and cleanup on call end.

title: BullMQ queue and worker Maps never cleaned up
description: Lines 153 and 205 maintain Maps of BullMQ Queue and Worker instances. These are created on demand but never cleaned up. Each Queue/Worker holds Redis connections. Over the lifetime of the process, if queue names change or workers are no longer needed, stale connections accumulate.
fix: Implement a cleanup function that closes and removes unused queues/workers. Register with graceful shutdown. Consider a queue.close() call before removing from the Map.

title: 52+ silent .catch(() => {}) error swallowing across codebase
description: Found 52+ instances of .catch(() => {}) that silently discard errors. Key locations: voip/call-control.ts (20+ instances including recording consent, scoring, summary generation), voip/coaching-engine.ts:358/363/374, voip/voice-ai-engine.ts:228/288, voip/training-conference.ts:359/364/409, voip/sms-engine.ts:298/442, voip/voip-state.ts:94/104/109/117/119, cache.ts:247/285/300/315, metrics.ts:104/105/107, session-security.ts:336, auth-config.ts:173, brute-force-protection.ts:105/208. While fire-and-forget is sometimes appropriate, completely discarding errors makes debugging impossible. Some of these suppress critical failures like recording consent logging, cache writes, and auth audit logs.
fix: Replace .catch(() => {}) with .catch((e) => logger.error('context', { error: e.message })) at minimum. For critical operations (auth audit, recording consent), consider a dead-letter queue or secondary logging mechanism.

title: JSON.parse called without try/catch in 30+ locations
description: Found 30+ JSON.parse calls without error handling: media/video-highlights.ts:132, media/seo-meta.ts:47, admin-audit.ts:267, email/automation-engine.ts:144/145, webhooks/outgoing.ts:202, translation/auto-translate.ts:318, api-handler.ts:72, email/inbound-handler.ts:613, audit-engine.ts:42/222, voip/agent-assist.ts:193/347, voip/transcription.ts:215/344, voip/live-scoring.ts:191, accounting/revenue-recognition.service.ts:97, voip/webhook-dispatcher.ts:330/334, accounting/period-close.service.ts:197, api/api-auth.middleware.ts:197/388. If any of these receive malformed JSON (corrupt DB data, truncated Redis values, malformed API responses), the JSON.parse throws an unhandled exception that crashes the request.
fix: Wrap all JSON.parse calls in try/catch with appropriate fallback values and error logging. Consider creating a safeParse utility: function safeParse<T>(json: string, fallback: T): T { try { return JSON.parse(json); } catch { return fallback; } }

title: 4 open TODO/FAILLE items in auth-config.ts — security debt
description: auth-config.ts has 4 unresolved security TODOs: FAILLE-086 (cookie prefix forced for Azure), FAILLE-091 (encryptedAdapter as any), FAILLE-092 (signOut doesn't log IP/user-agent), FAILLE-100 (already fixed). These represent known security gaps in the authentication system. FAILLE-092 is particularly concerning for NYDFS compliance: signOut events are logged without IP/user-agent, making suspicious logout tracing impossible.
fix: Address each FAILLE systematically: FAILLE-091 can be fixed with proper TypeScript types. FAILLE-092 requires accessing the request headers in the signOut event. FAILLE-086 needs review after Azure HTTPS E2E is configured.

title: 3 open TODO/FAILLE items in security.ts — security debt
description: security.ts has 3 unresolved TODOs: FAILLE-087 (setInterval timers not tracked — central timer registry needed), FAILLE-090 (email masking logic inconsistent between maskSensitiveData and admin-audit), FAILLE-093 (phoneSchema only accepts E.164 format). FAILLE-087 affects all timer-based cleanup across the codebase. FAILLE-090 means PII masking is inconsistent, potentially exposing data in some log paths.
fix: Create the central timer registry (FAILLE-087). Unify email masking into a single maskPII() function (FAILLE-090). Consider libphonenumber-js for phone validation (FAILLE-093).

title: SMS and push notification TODO stubs in email automation engine
description: Lines 425 and 434 have unimplemented TODO stubs for SMS and push notification actions in the email automation flow engine. If a user creates an automation flow with SMS or push notification nodes, they silently do nothing.
fix: Integrate with the existing sms.ts service for SMS and crm/push-notifications.ts for push. If not ready, throw an explicit error or log a warning when these action types are encountered.

title: JSON deep clone via JSON.parse(JSON.stringify()) — slow for large objects
description: Line 77 uses JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(params.metadata)) for deep cloning, which is the slowest deep clone method. Same pattern in search-analytics.ts:57 and platform/recording-import.ts:370. For large metadata objects, this causes unnecessary serialization overhead.
fix: Use structuredClone() (available in Node.js 17+) or a library like rfdc for efficient deep cloning.

title: CSRF token parsed from base64 with JSON.parse without try/catch
description: Line 98 parses a CSRF token from base64: JSON.parse(Buffer.from(encoded, 'base64').toString()). If the token is tampered with (invalid base64 or invalid JSON), this throws an unhandled exception. An attacker could craft a malformed CSRF token to cause a 500 error instead of the expected 403.
fix: Wrap in try/catch and return null/invalid for malformed tokens, matching the expected CSRF validation flow.

title: Webhook dispatcher uses $queryRaw for subscription lookup — could use Prisma findMany
description: Line 313 uses $queryRaw for what appears to be a standard query. Lines 330/334 then JSON.parse the raw results. Using the Prisma client directly would provide type safety and avoid manual JSON parsing.
fix: Replace the raw query with prisma.webhookEndpoint.findMany() with appropriate where/select clauses.

title: OpenAI SDK loaded via require() in transcription service
description: Line 37 uses require('openai') without type safety. The transcription service processes potentially sensitive audio content (customer calls with PII). Using untyped access to the OpenAI SDK increases the risk of misconfigured API calls.
fix: Use dynamic import() with proper typing: const { default: OpenAI } = await import('openai').

title: process.exit(1) in seed script could terminate the server
description: Line 83 calls process.exit(1) on error. If this module is accidentally imported by a server-side file (not just CLI), it would terminate the entire Node.js process.
fix: Replace process.exit(1) with throw new Error() so the error can be caught by callers. Only use process.exit in the top-level CLI entry point.

title: Translation queue uses setTimeout for job processing — not crash-resilient
description: Line 248 uses setTimeout for translation queue processing. Lines 607 and 699 add artificial delays. The entire translation queue is in-memory and lost on restart.
fix: Use BullMQ job queue for translation jobs to ensure crash resilience and distributed processing.

title: Workflow code sandbox uses setTimeout for timeout — potential CPU spin
description: Line 248: await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, 50)). This appears to be a polling delay in a sandbox execution context. Without proper timeout enforcement, a malicious workflow could consume CPU.
fix: Use proper sandboxing with vm2 or isolated-vm for workflow code execution. Enforce CPU time limits at the sandbox level.

title: Power dialer wrap-up timer stored as class property without cleanup tracking
description: Line 35: wrapUpTimer?: ReturnType<typeof setTimeout>. The timer reference is stored but if the session object is garbage collected before the timer fires, the callback will still execute with potentially stale state.
fix: Clear the wrapUpTimer in session cleanup/end functions. Track all active timers for cleanup on process shutdown.

title: Event listeners added to audio elements without cleanup
description: Lines 282 and 285 add 'loadedmetadata' and 'error' event listeners to audio elements. These listeners are never removed, which is a minor memory leak in client-side code (though the audio element will be garbage collected, the listeners may prevent collection if they reference closures).
fix: Store references to event listeners and call removeEventListener in a cleanup method, or use { once: true } option.

title: SMS campaign engine uses simple sleep for rate limiting
description: Line 37 defines a sleep function used for pacing SMS delivery. This blocks the async context for each message, making the entire campaign sequential. For large campaigns (1000+ messages), this could take hours.
fix: Use a proper rate-limited job queue (BullMQ with rate limiter) to process messages concurrently within rate limits.

title: API key permissions parsed from DB as JSON without validation
description: Lines 197 and 388 parse API key permissions from the database: JSON.parse(permissions) and JSON.parse(apiKeyRecord.permissions). If the permissions field is corrupted or contains unexpected values, the parsed array could grant unintended access.
fix: Validate the parsed permissions against the known PermissionCode enum. Reject any unknown permission codes. Add try/catch around the JSON.parse.

title: Enrichment pipeline uses 1-second sleep between batch items
description: Line 450: await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 1000)). A 1-second delay between enrichment API calls. For large batches, this creates very long processing times.
fix: Use concurrent processing with a concurrency limiter (e.g., p-limit) instead of sequential processing with fixed delays.

title: Three separate API guard implementations with overlapping logic
description: There are three separate guard/middleware files for API routes: admin-api-guard.ts, user-api-guard.ts, and mobile-guard.ts. Each implements its own rate limiting, auth checking, and error handling with slight variations. This triplication creates maintenance burden and inconsistency risk.
fix: Create a base guard factory function that accepts configuration (roles, rate limits, auth method) and generates the middleware. The three guards would then be thin configuration wrappers.

title: Chat realtime uses Redis pub/sub channel name without tenant prefix
description: Line 23: const CHAT_CHANNEL = 'chat:events'. In a multi-tenant system, all tenants share the same Redis pub/sub channel. This means chat events from one tenant could be received by another tenant's subscribers. The channel name should include the tenantId.
fix: Use tenant-prefixed channel names: `chat:${tenantId}:events`. The subscriber should only listen to its own tenant's channel.

title: SMS drip sequence and enrollment stores are volatile in-memory Maps
description: Lines 58-59: sequenceStore and enrollmentStore Maps store SMS drip campaign definitions and user enrollments. These are lost on restart, meaning users stop receiving their drip sequences and there's no way to resume them.
fix: Persist sequences and enrollments to the database. Use in-memory Maps only as a read-through cache.

title: Predictive dialer pre-connect sessions stored in unbounded Map
description: Line 103: const activePreConnects Map stores pre-connected call sessions. These consume Telnyx API resources (active phone calls). If the Map grows without bounds or sessions aren't properly cleaned up, it could result in orphaned phone calls billing the account.
fix: Add MAX_PRECONNECT_SESSIONS limit and automatic cleanup of sessions older than 30 seconds. Implement a circuit breaker that stops pre-connecting if too many sessions are active.

title: Post-call survey pending state stored in volatile Map
description: Line 65: const pendingSurveys Map stores surveys waiting to be sent after call completion. If the server restarts between call end and survey delivery, surveys are never sent.
fix: Persist pending surveys to the database or use a job queue with delayed execution.

title: Conference call state stored in volatile Map — active calls lost on restart
description: Line 47: const conferences Map stores active conference call state. If the server restarts during a conference, participants are orphaned with no way to reconnect them.
fix: Store conference state in Redis with TTL. On restart, query Telnyx API for active conferences to rebuild state.

title: ACD queue state stored in volatile Map — callers in queue lost on restart
description: Line 65: const queueState Map stores callers waiting in ACD queues. On restart, all queued callers are silently dropped. This is a critical service interruption for a contact center.
fix: Store queue state in Redis. On startup, check for orphaned callers and either re-queue them or route to voicemail.

title: Agentless dialer active calls stored without limit — billing risk
description: Line 43: const activeAgentlessCalls Map stores active calls made by the agentless dialer. Without a maximum limit, a bug could trigger unlimited outbound calls, resulting in massive Telnyx billing charges.
fix: Add MAX_CONCURRENT_CALLS limit (e.g., 20). Implement a circuit breaker that pauses the dialer if too many calls are active or failing.

title: web-vitals listed in dependencies but never imported in src/
description: The web-vitals package is in production dependencies but is never imported in any source file. This suggests it was added speculatively but never wired up for performance monitoring.
fix: Either implement web-vitals reporting (e.g., in a reportWebVitals function or via next/third-parties) or remove the dependency.

title: livekit-server-sdk listed in dependencies but never imported in src/
description: The livekit-server-sdk package is in production dependencies but search for 'from livekit-server-sdk' returns zero results. This appears to be unused dead weight.
fix: Remove livekit-server-sdk from dependencies if unused.

title: No staging environment — code deploys directly to production
description: The deploy workflow only deploys to 'production' by default. While there is a workflow_dispatch option for 'staging', no staging environment URL or configuration is defined. Every push to main goes straight to the live production site.
fix: Set up a staging Azure App Service slot. Deploy to staging first, run smoke tests, then swap slots for zero-downtime production deployment.

title: cancel-in-progress: true may break deployments mid-flight
description: The concurrency setting cancel-in-progress: true (line 50) means a new push to main will cancel an in-progress deployment. If the previous deployment has already applied Azure settings or pushed code but hasn't completed the health check, this can leave the app in an inconsistent state.
fix: Change to cancel-in-progress: false so deployments complete fully. If rapid iteration is needed, use a queue-based approach where new deploys wait for the current one to finish.

title: CodeQL init and analyze should be in separate steps with autobuild
description: The SAST job initializes CodeQL (line 85) and runs analyze (line 90) without an autobuild step between them. For JavaScript/TypeScript, CodeQL typically needs the code to be built to perform accurate analysis. The init step should be followed by 'actions/autobuild' before analyze.
fix: Add 'uses: github/codeql-action/autobuild@v3' between the init and analyze steps.

title: Playwright E2E tests only run weekly — not on PRs or pushes
description: The Playwright workflow runs on a weekly schedule (Monday 6 AM) and manual dispatch only. It does not run on pull requests or pushes. This means E2E regressions can be introduced and not caught for up to a week.
fix: Add 'pull_request: branches: [main]' trigger to run E2E tests on PRs. Use a matrix with a subset of tests for PR runs and the full suite for the weekly run.

title: Playwright tests run without starting the server in CI
description: The CI workflow builds the application but does not start the Next.js server before running 'npx playwright test'. The playwright.config.ts only starts the webServer when NOT in CI (process.env.CI ? undefined : { ... }). Without a running server, all E2E tests will fail with connection refused errors.
fix: Add a step before Playwright tests: 'npm run start &' with a wait-on script, or configure webServer in playwright.config.ts to also run in CI using 'npm run start'.

title: No global rate limiting in middleware — all routes unthrottled
description: The middleware.ts file handles auth, CORS, and security headers but has NO rate limiting logic. While individual routes (admin API, cron, webhooks) implement their own rate limiting via rate-limiter-flexible, there is no global rate limit to prevent DDoS or brute force attacks on unauthenticated endpoints like /auth/signin, /api/auth/*, or public APIs.
fix: Add a global rate limiter in middleware.ts using the existing rate-limiter-flexible package. Apply conservative limits (e.g., 100 req/min) for auth endpoints and generous limits (e.g., 1000 req/min) for public pages. Use x-forwarded-for or x-azure-clientip for client identification.

title: CORS Access-Control-Allow-Credentials header missing — cookies not sent cross-origin
description: The middleware sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin for API routes but never sets Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. If any frontend client needs to send authentication cookies cross-origin (e.g., from a custom domain to the API), the browser will block the cookies. Additionally, using a specific origin (not *) without credentials means the CORS setup may not match all client needs.
fix: If cross-origin cookie sending is needed, add 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true' to the CORS response headers. If not needed, document this decision.

title: Duplicate CSP definition in next.config.js and middleware.ts
description: The Content-Security-Policy is defined in both next.config.js (headers function, line 112-131) and middleware.ts (addSecurityHeaders function, line 37-54). These two definitions must be kept in sync manually. If one is updated without the other, different routes will have different security policies, creating inconsistencies.
fix: Define CSP directives in a single shared module (e.g., src/lib/csp.ts) and import it in both next.config.js and middleware.ts. This ensures a single source of truth.

title: React 18 pinned while Next.js 15 supports React 19 — missing concurrent features
description: React is pinned to ^18.2.0 while Next.js ^15.5.12 supports React 19. React 19 includes performance improvements (compiler, automatic memoization, use() hook, server components improvements) that Next.js 15 is designed to leverage.
fix: Upgrade to React 19 and react-dom 19. Test thoroughly as some third-party component libraries may need updates.

title: .env.example missing 20+ env vars used in production code
description: Multiple environment variables referenced in the codebase are not documented in .env.example: SENTRY_DSN, ZAPIER_API_KEY, ZAPIER_WEBHOOK_SECRET, SHIPPING_WEBHOOK_SECRET, TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN, META_APP_SECRET, TEAMS_WEBHOOK_SECRET, WEBEX_WEBHOOK_SECRET, STRIPE_ATTITUDES_WEBHOOK_SECRET, VOIP_CDR_WEBHOOK_SECRET (duplicate listing without value), EMAIL_WEBHOOK_SECRET, RESEND_WEBHOOK_SECRET, SITE_URL, AUTH_SECRET, WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_URL, FACEBOOK_APP_SECRET, and others. A developer setting up the project will not know these are needed until runtime errors occur.
fix: Audit all process.env.* references in src/ and add every variable to .env.example with a clear description and example value. Group by feature area.

title: GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID defined twice in .env.example
description: GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID and GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET appear twice in .env.example: once under 'OAUTH PROVIDERS' (lines 18-19) and again under 'PLATFORM INTEGRATIONS' (lines 96-97). This is confusing and could lead to different values being set for what should be the same credential.
fix: Remove the duplicate entries. Add a comment in the Platform Integrations section referencing the OAuth section: '# Google (Meet + YouTube): uses GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID and GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET above'.

title: No 'prepare' script for husky — hooks not auto-installed after npm install
description: Husky v8 requires a 'prepare' script in package.json to automatically install Git hooks after 'npm install'. Without it, developers who clone and install will not have pre-commit hooks active.
fix: Add '"prepare": "husky install"' to the scripts section of package.json.

title: No 'test:e2e' script — E2E tests not easily runnable
description: There is no 'test:e2e' script in package.json to run Playwright tests. Developers must know to run 'npx playwright test' manually. The 'test' script only runs Jest (which has no test files).
fix: Add '"test:e2e": "playwright test"' and '"test:all": "npm run test && npm run test:e2e"' to scripts.

title: Database backup has no retention policy or rotation
description: The backup-database.yml workflow runs twice daily but has no configuration for backup rotation or retention. Old backups will accumulate indefinitely in Azure Blob Storage, increasing storage costs. There is also no verification step to confirm the backup is valid (e.g., restoring to a test DB).
fix: Add a lifecycle policy to the Azure Blob Storage container to automatically delete backups older than 30 days. Add a weekly backup verification job that restores the latest backup to a temp database and runs a basic query.

title: Backup failure notification is echo-only — no real alerting
description: When the database backup fails, the workflow only outputs a GitHub Actions error annotation (echo '::error::'). There is no email, Slack, or PagerDuty notification. A failed backup could go unnoticed for days until a disaster requires restoration.
fix: Add a proper notification mechanism (Slack webhook, email) for backup failures. This is critical for a production database.

title: 34 cron jobs with no centralized scheduler or monitoring dashboard
description: There are 34 cron job endpoints under /api/cron/. Each uses CRON_SECRET for authentication and withJobLock for overlap prevention, which is good. However, there is no centralized scheduler configuration (like a crontab or Vercel cron config) and no monitoring dashboard to track execution history, success/failure rates, and latency. The admin has /admin/cron-stats but no automated alerting for cron failures.
fix: Create a centralized cron configuration file documenting all 34 jobs with their schedules. Implement cron execution logging to a database table (CronExecution: jobName, startedAt, finishedAt, status, error). Add an alert when a cron job fails 3 times consecutively.

title: Winston logger writes to filesystem — fails on Azure read-only deployments
description: The logger imports 'fs' and 'path' and likely writes log files to the filesystem. Per KB-PP-BUILD-006, Azure with WEBSITE_RUN_FROM_PACKAGE=1 mounts the deployment as read-only. File-based logging will fail silently or crash. Production logs should go to stdout/stderr for Azure App Service to capture.
fix: Ensure the production transport is Console only (stdout/stderr). Remove or conditionally disable any File transports when WEBSITE_RUN_FROM_PACKAGE=1 or NODE_ENV=production. Azure App Service captures stdout automatically.

title: Health check reports Sentry as 'not configured' — no APM in production
description: The health check at /api/health shows Sentry is not configured (SENTRY_DSN missing). While there are instrumentation files (src/instrumentation.ts, src/lib/apm.ts), the actual Sentry DSN is not set. This means there is no application performance monitoring, error tracking, or exception alerting in production.
fix: Set up a Sentry project and configure SENTRY_DSN in Azure App Service settings. Install @sentry/nextjs and configure the Sentry SDK properly for Next.js 15 App Router.

title: isomorphic-dompurify at release candidate (rc.2) in production dependencies
description: isomorphic-dompurify@3.0.0-rc.2 is a release candidate, not a stable release. Release candidates may have unresolved bugs. For a sanitization library that protects against XSS, stability is critical.
fix: Pin to the latest stable version of isomorphic-dompurify, or use dompurify (also in deps) directly with proper server/client handling.

title: Missing date-fns from optimizePackageImports may cause larger bundles
description: date-fns is listed in experimental.optimizePackageImports but is NOT in package.json dependencies. This import optimization has no effect. Meanwhile, packages like exceljs, bullmq, stripe, and openai that ARE used could benefit from tree-shaking optimization.
fix: Remove date-fns from optimizePackageImports since it is not a dependency. Add other heavy packages that would benefit from tree-shaking.

title: Prisma version in CI (5.22.0) may differ from package.json (^5.8.0)
description: The deploy workflow installs prisma@5.22.0 explicitly for schema sync (line 388), while package.json has ^5.8.0. This version mismatch could cause schema push to behave differently in CI vs local development.
fix: Align the Prisma version in the deploy workflow with the version in package.json. Better yet, use the already-installed version from npm ci instead of reinstalling.

title: No Dockerfile — manual Azure deployment only, no containerization option
description: There is no Dockerfile or docker-compose.yml in the project. The deployment relies entirely on Azure App Service with standalone build. This limits deployment options and makes local production testing difficult. The security-scan.yml even has a disabled container-scan job (if: false) waiting for a Dockerfile.
fix: Create a Dockerfile for the standalone Next.js build. This enables: local production testing, alternative hosting (Railway, Fly.io, AWS ECS), faster Azure deployments via container registry, and enables the container security scan in CI.

title: Testing libraries installed but unused — @testing-library/jest-dom and @testing-library/react
description: @testing-library/jest-dom and @testing-library/react are in devDependencies but with zero test files in src/__tests__/, they serve no purpose. This is not inherently harmful but indicates testing infrastructure was set up but never used.
fix: Either write tests using these libraries or remove them to reduce install time. Writing tests is strongly preferred.

title: @types/jest in devDependencies but jest-environment-jsdom version mismatch
description: @types/jest is at ^30.0.0 while jest itself is at ^29.7.0 and jest-environment-jsdom is at ^30.2.0. Version mismatches between Jest packages can cause subtle test failures or type errors.
fix: Align all Jest-related packages to the same major version. Either upgrade jest to v30 or downgrade @types/jest and jest-environment-jsdom to v29.

title: No integration test suite for API routes
description: There are no integration tests for the 200+ API routes. API routes handle critical business logic (payments, orders, accounting, CRM) and could have subtle bugs that unit tests miss but integration tests would catch (e.g., database constraints, auth middleware behavior, response shapes).
fix: Create an integration test suite using supertest or Next.js's test utilities. Priority routes: /api/payments/webhook, /api/admin/orders, /api/auth/*, /api/cron/*.

title: Order state machine has no unit tests — critical business logic untested
description: The order status machine (validateTransition) is used by multiple webhook handlers and API routes but has zero unit tests. An invalid state transition could allow orders to go from DELIVERED back to PENDING, or skip the SHIPPED state.
fix: Write exhaustive unit tests for validateTransition() covering all valid transitions, invalid transitions, and edge cases (terminal states, self-transitions).

title: Financial calculation library has no unit tests — rounding errors possible
description: The decimal-calculator library (using decimal.js) handles money arithmetic for the entire platform. Functions like multiply, add, subtract, applyRate are used in checkout, accounting, and tax calculations. Zero unit tests means rounding errors or precision bugs could cause financial discrepancies.
fix: Write comprehensive unit tests for all decimal-calculator functions with edge cases: very large numbers, very small fractions, rounding modes, currency-specific precision.

title: Brute force protection has no tests — security mechanism unverified
description: The brute force protection module is used in authentication flows but has no unit tests. If the protection fails silently, attackers could attempt unlimited login attempts without being blocked.
fix: Write tests verifying: lockout after N failed attempts, lockout duration, reset after successful login, IP-based vs account-based lockout, Redis fallback behavior.

title: No disk space check in health endpoint — Azure storage could fill
description: The health check monitors memory but not disk space. On Azure App Service, the /home directory has limited storage. If uploads, logs, or temp files fill the disk, the application will crash without the health check detecting it.
fix: Add a disk space check using fs.statfs('/home') or equivalent. Warn at 80% usage, fail at 95%.

title: Request logging uses Math.random() for sampling — not cryptographically random
description: The middleware uses Math.random() < 0.1 for log sampling (line 247). While not a security vulnerability (it's just for sampling rate), Math.random() is predictable. More importantly, the sampling rate (10%) is hardcoded with no configuration option.
fix: Make the sampling rate configurable via an environment variable (e.g., LOG_SAMPLING_RATE=0.1). The use of Math.random() for sampling is acceptable but consider using crypto.getRandomValues for consistency with the rest of the security-conscious codebase.

title: No request duration tracking in middleware — no latency observability
description: The middleware generates a request start time (line 251) but never calculates or reports request duration. There is no way to identify slow requests or measure P50/P95/P99 latency without APM (Sentry is not configured).
fix: Set x-request-start header with Date.now() for downstream duration calculation, or implement duration tracking via a response interceptor. Better yet, configure Sentry APM for automatic request duration tracking.

title: Cron jobs have no timeout — can run indefinitely consuming resources
description: While the cron-lock module has a DEFAULT_MAX_DURATION_MS of 5 minutes with AbortController timeout, individual cron jobs can override this with arbitrarily long timeouts or disable timeout protection entirely. If a cron job enters an infinite loop or deadlock, it will consume server resources indefinitely.
fix: Enforce a hard maximum timeout (e.g., 10 minutes) in the withJobLock wrapper that cannot be overridden. Log and alert when jobs approach the timeout limit.

title: REDIS_URL is optional but critical for rate limiting, cron locks, and idempotency
description: REDIS_URL is listed as optional in .env.example, but the application relies on Redis for: rate limiting (21 files), cron job locks (distributed), webhook idempotency (all webhook endpoints), and session caching. Without Redis, all these features degrade to in-memory fallbacks that don't work across multiple instances and lose state on restart.
fix: Document Redis as a required dependency for production deployments. Add a health check warning when Redis is unavailable in production. Consider failing fast in production if Redis is not configured.

title: Unknown domain defaults to 'biocycle' tenant — should return error for unknown hosts
description: The resolveTenantFromHost function defaults any unknown hostname to { tenantSlug: 'biocycle', isSuperAdmin: false } (line 184). This means any random domain pointing to the server will serve the BioCycle tenant content. In a multi-tenant SaaS, unknown domains should return a 404 or redirect to the main site.
fix: For unknown hostnames, return a dedicated error page or redirect to attitudes.vip. Log the unknown hostname for monitoring purposes.

title: In-memory rate limiting on webhooks does not work across multiple instances
description: Both shipping webhook and email-bounce webhook use in-memory rate limiting (Map/object in process memory). The email-bounce route even documents this limitation (line 20-21): 'This state lives in the Node.js process memory and is NOT distributed.' If Azure scales to multiple instances, each instance has its own counter, effectively multiplying the rate limit.
fix: Migrate webhook rate limiting to Redis-backed rate limiting using the existing rate-limiter-flexible package. This ensures rate limits work correctly across multiple instances.

title: secureCookie: false for auth session — cookies sent over HTTP
description: The getToken call uses secureCookie: false (line 358). The comment explains this is because Azure TLS terminates at the load balancer, so the cookie doesn't have the __Secure- prefix. However, this also means the session cookie can be transmitted over unencrypted HTTP connections during development, making it vulnerable to session hijacking on non-HTTPS connections.
fix: Set secureCookie to process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production' or to true when HSTS is enforced. The cookie name 'authjs.session-token' should still work with Azure's TLS termination. Alternatively, ensure HSTS (already configured) prevents any HTTP access.

title: WhatsApp webhook swallows errors and returns 200 — silent failures
description: The WhatsApp webhook catch block (line 135-141) logs the error but returns { success: true } with 200 status. The comment says this prevents Twilio retries, but it also means legitimate processing errors are silently swallowed. The webhook will appear successful even when message processing completely fails.
fix: Track error counts and alert when error rate exceeds a threshold. Consider returning 500 for non-transient errors and 200 only for duplicate/already-processed messages. Twilio retry behavior can be controlled via the webhook configuration.

title: No rollback mechanism — failed deployments require manual intervention
description: The deploy pipeline has no automated rollback capability. If a deployment passes the health check but causes runtime issues (e.g., specific pages crash, API errors), there is no way to automatically revert to the previous version. The only option is a manual git revert + new deployment (13+ minutes).
fix: Implement Azure deployment slots: deploy to a staging slot first, run smoke tests, then swap to production. If post-swap health checks fail, auto-swap back. This gives instant rollback capability.

title: CartItem has no FK relation for productId and optionId
description: CartItem has productId and optionId fields but no @relation to Product or ProductOption. No referential integrity enforcement.
fix: Add product Product @relation(fields: [productId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade) and option ProductOption? @relation(fields: [optionId], references: [id], onDelete: SetNull).

title: ChatSettings is a singleton with no FK relation to User for lastUpdatedBy
description: ChatSettings has lastUpdatedBy String? without FK relation. No referential integrity.
fix: Add @relation to User or document as intentional.

title: QuantityDiscount missing composite index for pricing queries
description: QuantityDiscount is queried by (productId, minQuantity) for price calculation but lacks a composite index.
fix: Add @@index([productId, minQuantity]).

title: UserDevice.token has both @unique and @@index([token]), redundant
description: UserDevice has token @unique AND @@index([token]). The @unique constraint already creates a unique index.
fix: Remove @@index([token]).

title: ApiKey.keyHash has both @unique and @@index([keyHash]), redundant
description: ApiKey has keyHash @unique AND @@index([keyHash]). The @unique constraint already creates a unique index.
fix: Remove @@index([keyHash]).

title: Ambassador.referralCode has both @unique and @@index, redundant
description: Ambassador has referralCode @unique AND @@index([referralCode]). Redundant index.
fix: Remove @@index([referralCode]).

title: ProductTierPrice missing composite index for pricing lookups
description: ProductTierPrice is queried by (productId, tierId) but relies on separate indexes. A composite would be more efficient.
fix: Add @@index([productId, tierId]) or better: @@unique([productId, tierId, optionId]).

title: ProductView grows unbounded without TTL
description: ProductView records every product page visit. At scale, this table grows rapidly without cleanup.
fix: Implement 90-day retention or aggregate into daily counts after 30 days.

title: ApiUsageLog grows unbounded without TTL
description: ApiUsageLog records every API key usage. High-volume table without cleanup strategy.
fix: Implement 30-day retention or aggregate into hourly/daily counts.

title: AureliaChat grows unbounded without cleanup strategy
description: AureliaChat stores AI chat conversations. Without cleanup, this table grows indefinitely.
fix: Implement retention policy: archive chats older than 6 months.

title: 33 routes have zero security controls (no auth, CSRF, rate limit, or secret)
description: 33 routes have absolutely no security mechanism. Most are legitimately public (product listings, search, FAQ, health check), but several should have protections: /api/public/chat, /api/public/chatbot, /api/demo-request, /api/tracking/click, /api/tracking/email, /api/mailing-list/confirm, /api/consent/[token], /api/estimates/[token], /api/data-deletion, /api/admin/tutoriels/[...slug], /api/voip/debug, /api/community/debug. Full list: bundles/*, client-references, contact/platforms, currencies, faq, guides, hero-slides/active, payment-methods, products/related, products/by-slug, products/compare, products/recommendations, search/popular, search/suggest, testimonials, videos/*, webinars.
fix: Add at minimum rate limiting to all 33 routes. Add authentication to debug routes and admin routes. Add CSRF to any that accept POST.

title: VoIP CDR ingest allows dev-mode bypass of webhook authentication
description: The /api/admin/voip/cdr/ingest route (line 40-46) returns true when VOIP_CDR_WEBHOOK_SECRET is not set AND NODE_ENV is not 'production'. This means in staging/preview environments, any unauthenticated request can inject CDR data. The survey submit route has the same pattern.
fix: Remove the dev-mode bypass. Require webhook secret in all environments. Use a .env file with a dev secret for local development.

title: JSON.parse without try/catch in 10 accounting routes risks unhandled exceptions
description: 10 instances of JSON.parse() in accounting routes are not wrapped in their own try/catch blocks. While the outer route handler may have a try/catch, malformed JSON stored in database fields (notes, resultData, errorLog, inputData, config) will cause the JSON.parse to throw, potentially interrupting the response with an unhelpful error. Files: src/app/api/accounting/tax-reports/route.ts (lines 312, 330), src/app/api/accounting/batch/[id]/route.ts (lines 34, 40, 46), src/app/api/accounting/reports/custom/route.ts, src/app/api/accounting/reports/custom/[id]/route.ts, src/app/api/accounting/reports/custom/[id]/run/route.ts.
fix: Wrap each JSON.parse in a try/catch with a sensible fallback (empty object or null). Create a safeParse helper function.

title: Product import route spreads data into Prisma create
